title: C Strings
content:
### Strings in C

C does not have a dedicated string type. Strings are represented as arrays of characters terminated by a null character (`\0`).

### String Declaration

```c
char str1[6] = {'H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', '\0'};
char str2[] = "Hello";  // size 6 (includes null terminator)
char *str3 = "Hello";   // string literal (read-only)
```

### Null Terminator

Every C string ends with a null character `\0` (ASCII value 0). This marks the end of the string.

| String | Memory Content |
|--------|---------------|
| `"Hi"` | `'H' 'i' '\0'` |
| `"C"` | `'C' '\0'` |
| Empty `""` | `'\0'` |

### Important Notes

- Always allocate one extra byte for the null terminator
- String literals are stored in read-only memory (modifying them is undefined behavior)
- Character arrays with `char name[]` can be modified
- Pointer to string literal (`char *p = "hello"`) cannot be safely modified

---

> **Key Point:** Always ensure enough space for the null terminator `\0`. A string of length n needs an array of size n+1.

quiz.question: What character terminates all C strings?
